episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [Williams Lake Tribune] Adam Sandler spotted in B.C. while filming movie and [Williams Lake Tribune] Robert Barron column: I hope I get to see humans walk on Mars, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How should rural and Indigenous communities build digital cultural infrastructure that serves their storytelling needs while avoiding both corporate platform gatekeeping and unsustainable technological dependence? Topics include: Ireland's government digital wallet age verification system and identity gatekeeping implications, Algorithmic attention economics as a form of cultural gatekeeping versus government-mandated identity systems, Data sovereignty and Indigenous control over cultural archival materials, Regional digital cultural infrastructure co-op model with shared hosting and technical capacity, Digital preservation sustainability and technology obsolescence risks for cultural projects.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

discussion.central_question: How should rural and Indigenous communities build digital cultural infrastructure that serves their storytelling needs while avoiding both corporate platform gatekeeping and unsustainable technological dependence?
discussion.riley_position: Current platform algorithms are inherently hostile to place-based community storytelling, so communities should build outside these systems using open-source tools and community-owned hosting, which enables sustainable experiential digital storytelling.
riley_key_evidence: Photographic archives and oral histories don't generate engagement signals needed for platform visibility
The Doig River First Nation built a language and culture archive from community initiative without major institutional partnership
Open-source tools and publicly documented build processes create better durability than contractor-built custom experiences
discussion.casey_position: While platform gatekeeping is problematic, building independent infrastructure requires sustained capacity and governance that most small communities lack, and many promising digital cultural projects have been abandoned due to technology lifecycle obsolescence and one-person dependency.
casey_key_evidence: Mukurtu platform and Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami language tools required university partnerships or national funding infrastructure
Doig River project faced years of sustainability uncertainty and founder burnout risks
Multiple BC heritage projects became orphaned when underlying technologies (like Flash Player) became obsolete
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey reached substantial agreement that the current platform architecture is structurally harmful to rural/Indigenous storytelling and that building outside platforms with Indigenous co-design (not just consultation) is necessary, though Casey remains cautious about infrastructure-first approaches without proven sustainability models.
topics_covered: Ireland's government digital wallet age verification system and identity gatekeeping implications
Algorithmic attention economics as a form of cultural gatekeeping versus government-mandated identity systems
Data sovereignty and Indigenous control over cultural archival materials
Regional digital cultural infrastructure co-op model with shared hosting and technical capacity
Digital preservation sustainability and technology obsolescence risks for cultural projects
